narrative_blocks.header: Born three months after his father died
narrative_blocks.body: His father Matthew Hart died on October 30, 1736; he was born January 23, 1737 — a posthumous child. He grew up in Kensington and joined the church there in October 1760 at age twenty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Son's marriage created double Hooker descent
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Hart III married Urania Hooker — both Hooker descendants. Their grandchildren carried double descent from Thomas Hooker through different family lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed his household to New York around 1800
narrative_blocks.body: Around the turn of the nineteenth century he sold the Sheldon Moore homestead in Kensington and moved to Madison County, New York — to either Whitestown or De Ruyter. He died there around 1811, more than a decade after the move.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became America's foremost 1820s poet
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson James Gates Percival (1795-1856) — son of daughter Elizabeth and Dr. James Percival Jr. — became the most recognized American poet of the 1820s and Connecticut's first state geologist. James was born in Kensington before Matthew Hart Jr. departed for New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Born after his father had died
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Hart was born in January 1737, weeks after his father died the previous October. He grew up to serve as a Revolutionary corporal and later removed to New York.

cross_connections.display_label: His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.
His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.

narrative_blocks.header: Born three months after his father died
narrative_blocks.body: His father Matthew Hart died on October 30, 1736; he was born January 23, 1737 — a posthumous child. He grew up in Kensington and joined the church there in October 1760 at age twenty-three.

narrative_blocks.header: Son's marriage created double Hooker descent
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Hart III married Urania Hooker — both Hooker descendants. Their grandchildren carried double descent from Thomas Hooker through different family lines.

narrative_blocks.header: Removed his household to New York around 1800
narrative_blocks.body: Around the turn of the nineteenth century he sold the Sheldon Moore homestead in Kensington and moved to Madison County, New York — to either Whitestown or De Ruyter. He died there around 1811, more than a decade after the move.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became America's foremost 1820s poet
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson James Gates Percival (1795-1856) — son of daughter Elizabeth and Dr. James Percival Jr. — became the most recognized American poet of the 1820s and Connecticut's first state geologist. James was born in Kensington before Matthew Hart Jr. departed for New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Born after his father had died
narrative_blocks.body: Matthew Hart was born in January 1737, weeks after his father died the previous October. He grew up to serve as a Revolutionary corporal and later removed to New York.

cross_connections.display_label: Urania Hooker married Matthew Hart — continuing the Hart-Hooker intermarriage.
Urania Hooker married Matthew Hart — continuing the Hart-Hooker intermarriage.
Urania Hooker married Matthew Hart — continuing the Hart-Hooker intermarriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1784; ordained by Bishop Seabury; Hempstead rector
narrative_blocks.detail: He served as agent for the Connecticut Land Company (1796-1797) surveying lands in Cleveland, Ohio. Among his boarding school students around 1809 was his nephew James Gates Percival (X01936) — son of his sister Elizabeth Hart (H00610) — who later became a noted poet, Connecticut state geologist, and Wisconsin state geologist, writing his final report from memory without notes. The Hofstra University library holds the Hart-Nichols family papers, including correspondence compiled by Seth's granddaughter Mary Amelia Hart (1838-1932). His portrait was painted by Edwin Percival (nephew James Gates's brother) around 1829. (Hart Genealogy 1948; Hofstra Hart-Nichols Family Papers)
narrative_blocks.body: Seth Hart graduated Yale in 1784 and was ordained by Bishop Samuel Seabury — deacon in 1791, priest in 1792. He served as rector of St. George's Church in Hempstead, Long Island for 28 years (1800-1829), until paralysis forced his resignation. He died in 1832, 'much beloved and lamented by his former parishioners.'

narrative_blocks.header: His boarding school launched nephew Percival's poetry
narrative_blocks.body: He ran a boarding school at Hempstead where his nephew James Gates Percival was sent in 1807 after their father's death. Despite Percival's resentment of his uncle's 'amiable, cheerful, almost jovial' efforts to draw him out, the Hempstead years were where Percival first wrote poetry.

narrative_blocks.header: Officiated Cleveland's first baptism, marriage, and burial
narrative_blocks.body: In spring 1797 he went to the Western Reserve of Ohio as General Agent and Chaplain of the surveying party sent by the Connecticut Land Company to develop their settlement at Cleveland. He spent six months in service and officiated at the first baptism, the first marriage, and the first burial occurring in Cleveland.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale 1784; rector St. George's Hempstead 28 years

cross_connections.display_label: His boarding school was where nephew Percival first wrote poetry
His portrait painted by his nephew Edwin Percival — a Hooker descendant

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale 1784; became Episcopal rector at Hempstead LI for 28 years.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Episcopal rector at Hempstead, Long Island

career.organization: St. George's Church, Hempstead, Long Island
career.notes: Earlier rector of St. John's, Waterbury and St. Paul's, Wallingford. Yale 1784; ordained by Bishop Seabury.

parents.father_research_notes: Hon. Benjamin Hall of Cheshire CT

notable.notable_blurb: Columbia 1811; ordained deacon by Bishop Hobart; rector

institution_affiliations.notes: No Yale connection; graduated Columbia College 1811

career.organization: St. Andrew's Church, Walden, New York
career.notes: Columbia College 1811; principal of the Protestant Episcopal High School, New York, from 1828.

cross_connections.display_label: Her first cousin Edwin Percival (a 6th-gen Hooker descendant) painted her portrait in 1828 (ART027)

narrative_blocks.header: Followed the seas, then built 'Heartsease'
narrative_blocks.detail: The genealogical records of the Hart-Nichols family were primarily compiled by Benjamin's daughter Mary Amelia Hart (1838-1932), who lived to 94 and whose correspondence and research notes are preserved in the Hart-Nichols Family Papers at Hofstra University. The collection covers families including Nichols, Hall, Hart, Lefferts, Dalziel, Dorland, Stuart Smyth, Knowles, Searing, Lyman, Wood and Moore. (Hofstra Hart-Nichols Family Papers)
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Hall Hart (1811-1875) went to sea before settling down to marry Elizabeth Nichols of Hempstead in October 1837. He purchased a farm in LaGrange, Dutchess County, New York in 1838, establishing it as one of New York State's first commercial apple orchards. The Greek revival farmhouse was named 'Heartsease.' After the Civil War — during which his sons Walter Nichols Hart and Ambrose Burnham Hart served in the Union Army, Ambrose rising to First Lieutenant and Adjutant, wounded at Winchester, Virginia — Benjamin and his three sons purchased lands in Federal Point and Lake City, Florida during Reconstruction.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of poet-geologist James Gates Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart Percival's son James Gates Percival (born September 15, 1795) became a major American poet and the official state geologist of both Connecticut and Wisconsin after his Yale 1815 graduation. Elizabeth herself was described as 'by nature of a susceptible and delicate organization' who 'seems to have imparted to her son these qualities, with a tendency to excessive mental development.' She was understood to have transmitted depression and extreme sensitivity to her sons — James also suffered from depression throughout his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost husband and daughter within five weeks, 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth's first husband Dr. James Percival Jr. died January 22, 1807 in Kensington Connecticut at age forty, and five weeks later her daughter Harriet died February 16, 1807 at age seventeen. The compressed loss of husband and eldest child within five weeks marked the family's catastrophic early-1807. Elizabeth waited five years before remarrying — to Samuel Porter on April 6, 1812.

narrative_blocks.header: Son James worked Porter farm before re-entering Yale
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1812 second marriage to Samuel Porter, Elizabeth's son James Gates Percival worked on Porter's farm before re-entering Yale to complete his 1815 degree. The interim farm labor between the family rebuild and resumption of Yale studies was the kind of practical stop-gap that interrupted many early-Republic professional-track educations after parental deaths reset family finances.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Kensington 1769, four children with Dr. Percival
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hart was born in 1769 in Kensington Connecticut (baptized December 24, 1769) and married Dr. James Percival Jr. there in January 1790 at age twenty. Four children survived to be documented: Harriet (died 1807 age 17), Edwin (died Troy NY), James Gates Percival (Yale 1815 poet-geologist), and Oswin Hart Percival (baptized 1809, died never married at a New Haven hospital). Elizabeth herself died around 1846.

notable.notable_blurb: Busy country physician of Kensington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: He rode to patients across Kensington parish
narrative_blocks.body: For nineteen years Dr. Percival kept one of the busiest country practices in central Connecticut, making his calls on horseback except in deep winter. A contemporary said few country physicians ever did more business in so short a time.

narrative_blocks.header: He died suddenly at forty in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Percival died suddenly in January 1807, and his daughter Harriet within the same month. The double loss deeply marked his eleven-year-old son, the future poet James Gates Percival.

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT (41.62805, -72.76927) per FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: His memory shaped his brother’s biography
narrative_blocks.body: When Julius Ward wrote the 1866 life of the poet James Gates Percival, he leaned on Oswin’s recollections — including that James had tried to overdose on opium in 1820. Oswin was supplying memories gathered fifty years after the fact.

narrative_blocks.header: A boarding-school exile after his father died
narrative_blocks.body: When their father died in 1807, he was sent with his brother James to their uncle Rev. Seth Hart’s school at Hempstead on Long Island. He never married, and died around 1871 in the New Haven hospital; a painter who knew the brothers said they “never seemed to belong to this world.”

notable.notable_blurb: Portrait painter of his Hooker-descended kin

cross_connections.display_label: Painted his uncle and cousin — all three Hooker descendants
Painted his cousin in 1828 — both Hooker descendants

narrative_blocks.header: He painted his own Hooker-descended kin
narrative_blocks.body: A portrait painter of the Hart family of Kensington, Percival painted his uncle Rev. Seth Hart and his aunt Elizabeth around 1828 and 1829. Both sitter and painter descended from Thomas Hooker, a nephew rendering his uncle.

narrative_blocks.header: His brother was a famous poet-geologist
narrative_blocks.body: Edwin was a brother of James Gates Percival, the noted poet and state geologist of Connecticut. The Percivals of Kensington were a remarkably bookish and artistic family.

sources.short: Yale Archives — James Gates Percival Papers

notable.notable_blurb: America's foremost 1820s poet; first Connecticut geologist

narrative_blocks.header: Seven years mapping Connecticut at two-mile intervals
narrative_blocks.body: From 1835 to 1842 he conducted Connecticut's first state geological survey, traversing the state at two-mile intervals over seven years. The survey's accuracy is still cited by earth scientists; he was appointed Wisconsin state geologist in 1854 until his death two years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Foremost American poet of the 1820s
narrative_blocks.body: His 1821 'Prometheus' and 1822 'Clio' established him as the most recognized American poet of the 1820s. He was a key figure in early American Romantic literature before turning to lexicography and geology.

narrative_blocks.header: Edited Noah Webster's American Dictionary in 1828
narrative_blocks.body: Noah Webster recruited him in 1827 to edit the American Dictionary of the English Language. The 1828 first edition — Webster's foundational work — owed much of its etymological precision to Percival's editorial labor.

narrative_blocks.header: Entered Yale at sixteen, graduated head of class
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale College at sixteen and graduated at the head of his class four years later in 1815. He earned a medical degree from Yale Medical Institution in 1820 and briefly practiced surgery before turning to writing.

narrative_blocks.header: Suicide attempts and the long poem 'The Suicide'
narrative_blocks.body: In 1820 he suffered a severe mental health crisis after his father's death, making multiple attempts on his own life. He survived, withdrew from society, and channeled his suffering into 'The Suicide' — a long, intense poem from that period.

cross_connections.display_label: Uncle's boarding school — where he first wrote poetry at 14
cross_connections.description: Rev. Seth Hart ran the Hempstead boarding school where Percival and his brother Edwin were sent after their father's death in 1807. Percival was 11-12; Seth was 'amiable, cheerful, almost jovial' — a personality in sharp contrast to his melancholy nephew. Seth tried to get Percival to break out of his shell. According to later accounts, Percival resented this deeply, and directed verses of 'The Suicide' toward his uncle. The relationship was formative and fraught — Seth Hart was the first to witness Percival's literary voice emerge, but also the person Percival blamed for his suffering.

cross_connections.display_label: She asked him to edit her atlas; acknowledged they were cousins
cross_connections.description: Emma Willard and James Gates Percival were second cousins once removed — both 6th-generation Hooker descendants through separate branches of the Hart family, neither knowing of the shared Hooker ancestry. They corresponded 1829-1833: Emma asked Percival to review her Woodbridge & Willard geography textbook in 1829. In 1832 she wrote: 'we are distant relations, your mother having been of the same name and stock as my father... I knew and loved your father — he was our family physician.' Letters held at Yale Beinecke, YCAL MSS 703.

cross_connections.display_label: mapped as geologist the mine Hart secured for 1775
cross_connections.description: The Kensington lead mine on the Mill River — co-owned by Percival's father, half a mile from his childhood home — was managed during the Revolution by Gen. Selah Hart, appointed by the General Court in 1775 to secure lead ore for the colony. Percival surveyed the same mine as Connecticut state geologist, finding galena ore. Two Hooker descendants, two centuries, one mine. (CT Historical Society; Berlin-Peck Memorial Library)

cross_connections.display_label: Both edited Webster's Dictionary — Percival in 1826, Porter in 1865
cross_connections.description: James Gates Percival was effectively the principal editor of Noah Webster's American Dictionary of the English Language in 1826-1828 — 'feeling like the living tied to the dead,' he wrote of Webster. Rev. Noah Porter III edited later editions (1865). Both were connected through Farmington's Hooker-Cowles network: Percival through his mother Elizabeth Hart, granddaughter of Matthew Hart Jr. × Sarah Hooker; Porter through his father's 60-year Farmington ministry and the Cowles family.

cross_connections.display_label: Edited Noah Webster's 1828 American Dictionary
cross_connections.description: Percival was hired by Noah Webster as one of the editors of the 1828 American Dictionary of the English Language and its 1841 revision. Percival's exacting standards were a significant factor in the dictionary's reputation for scholarly accuracy. Both men were Connecticut-born; Webster (West Hartford 1758) was an orbit figure to the Hooker tree; Percival (Berlin CT 1795) descended from Thomas Hooker through six generations.

cross_connections.display_label: Governor Edwards appointed him state geologist 1835

education.institution_blurb: Graduated Yale 1815 at the head of his class; later earned M.D. at Yale.
Earned M.D. at Yale; his medical examination was legendarily rigorous.

open_questions.question: Identity of unnamed Hart daughter (H00612)
open_questions.notes: EH entry 612: 'Daughter, born ___; single. Lived with her sister, Mrs. Percival.' This is a sibling of Elizabeth Hart Percival (H00610) — one of Matthew Hart Jr.'s daughters who never married and lived with Elizabeth. Not to be confused with Harriet Percival (X01940), who was Elizabeth's daughter and died in 1807 age 17. No name found in Hart Genealogy 1948, EH, or FindAGrave. FindAGrave lists a 'Timothy Percival (1776-1808)' as a child of Elizabeth Hart — likely a FindAGrave error (Elizabeth was born 1769; a child born 1776 at age 7 is implausible). Candidate sources: Kensington church records, Berlin probate.
open_questions.action: Check Kensington Congregational Church records and Berlin CT probate for unnamed Hart daughter

death.cause: Died before posthumous son Matthew (H00286) was born Jan 23, 1737

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. John Hart (b. Apr 2, 1655, d. Nov 11, 1714)

burial.plot_notes: Christian Lane Cemetery, Kensington, Connecticut — same cemetery as Samuel Hooker (H00086)

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Matthew Hart married I00030's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Matthew Hart married I00030's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Sarah Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Sarah Hooker married I00030 in 1726.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Sarah Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Sarah Hooker married I00030 in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

parents.notes: Son of John Hart (X01887) who perished in the 1666 fire

cross_connections.display_label: married his son Nathaniel in 1719

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID: 77180356.

narrative_blocks.header: Away tending stock the night his family died
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1666 a fire set to the Hart house in Farmington killed everyone inside; John, then about eleven, survived only because he had been sent that night to Nod to tend the family's stock. He grew up the sole survivor of his household.

narrative_blocks.header: Three grandsons wed three of Hooker’s granddaughters
narrative_blocks.body: John's grandfather, Deacon Stephen Hart, had served Rev. Thomas Hooker's own church in Hartford. A generation later, three of John's grandsons — the brothers Matthew, Samuel, and Nathaniel Hart — married three granddaughters of Thomas Hooker, knotting the two families together within a few years.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandfather’s will singled the orphan out
narrative_blocks.body: When Deacon Stephen Hart wrote his will in 1682, he named the fire's lone survivor directly as his eldest son's son. The boy who had lost everyone kept his place in the family record and a small legacy.

cross_connections.display_label: Their families united — three sons married three Hooker daughters.

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.
cross_connections.display_label: helped govern Farmington alongside him for decades
married a descendant of both Hooker and Standley
served Hartford’s founding-family public life alongside him
was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother
fathered four children who married four of his own
Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered
signed a Tunxis dispossession deed as his descendant’s in-law
shared his entanglement in Tunxis affairs, decades apart
was called with him one of Farmington’s two prominent men

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

His son Matthew Hart Jr. married Urania Hooker — both.
